GS3.imageDescription: Conceptual image of ISRO's RLV-TD descending towards a runway.
GS3.trivia: ISRO's first successful orbital re-entry experiment, the Space Capsule Recovery Experiment (SRE-1), was conducted in 2007.

GS3.headline: Government Launches Enhanced National Program for Urban Air Quality Improvement
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change launched an enhanced phase of the National Clean Air Programme (NCAP) focusing on 131 non-attainment cities.
• The revised program includes stricter emission norms for industries, promotion of electric vehicles, and improved waste management practices.
• Special emphasis on real-time air quality monitoring networks and public awareness campaigns to foster behavioral change.
• The program aims for a 40% reduction in particulate matter concentration by 2026, building on previous targets.
• Inter-ministerial coordination and state-level implementation strategies are crucial for the success of this ambitious environmental initiative.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Disaster and disaster management.
Government Policies and Interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.
prelimsPoints: Enhanced National Clean Air Programme (NCAP) launched.
Focus on 131 non-attainment cities.
Aims for 40% reduction in particulate matter by 2026.
Includes stricter emission norms and EV promotion.
mainsPoints: Discuss the major sources of urban air pollution in India and their adverse impacts on public health and the environment.
Analyze the effectiveness of existing policies and programs, including NCAP, in tackling the air quality crisis.
Suggest innovative and multi-sectoral approaches for achieving significant improvements in urban air quality.
Examine the role of citizen participation and technological interventions in successful air pollution control strategies.
GS3.imageDescription: Skyline of an Indian city with a visible layer of smog, highlighting air pollution.
GS3.trivia: According to the World Health Organization (WHO), air pollution is the greatest environmental threat to human health globally.

GS3.headline: NITI Aayog Releases Draft 'Ethical AI Guidelines' for Public Consultation
GS3.explainer:
• NITI Aayog has published a comprehensive draft of 'Ethical AI Guidelines' for public feedback, aiming to foster responsible AI development and deployment in India.
• The guidelines emphasize principles of fairness, transparency, accountability, safety, privacy, and inclusivity in AI systems.
• They address critical areas such as data governance, algorithmic bias, human oversight, and the societal impact of AI technologies.
• The initiative seeks to position India as a leader in ethical AI, ensuring that technological advancements benefit all sections of society without exacerbating inequalities.
• These guidelines are crucial for establishing a robust regulatory framework for AI, balancing innovation with societal well-being.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
Awareness in the fields of Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights.
Government Policies and Interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation.

Principles include fairness, transparency, accountability, safety, privacy.
Addresses algorithmic bias and human oversight.
Aims for responsible AI development and deployment.
mainsPoints: Discuss the ethical challenges and societal implications of rapidly advancing Artificial Intelligence technologies.
Analyze the key principles outlined in NITI Aayog's draft 'Ethical AI Guidelines' and their importance for responsible AI deployment.
Examine the role of government and regulatory bodies in shaping the future of AI to ensure inclusivity and minimize risks.
Evaluate how India can become a global leader in ethical AI, balancing innovation with the protection of human values.
GS3.imageDescription: Abstract image representing artificial intelligence with interconnected nodes and human hand reaching out.
GS3.trivia: NITI Aayog, formed in 2015, replaced the Planning Commission and serves as the premier policy 'think tank' of the Government of India.

GS3.headline: New Cyber Security Framework for Critical Information Infrastructure Unveiled
GS3.explainer:
• The National Critical Information Infrastructure Protection Centre (NCIIPC) has released a new, updated cyber security framework for critical infrastructure sectors.
• The framework mandates stringent security audits, real-time threat intelligence sharing, and enhanced incident response protocols for entities in power, finance, and telecommunications.
• It aims to bolster India's cyber resilience against state-sponsored attacks and sophisticated cybercrime syndicates.
• Emphasis on indigenization of cyber security products and services to reduce reliance on foreign vendors.
• Training and capacity building for cyber security professionals across critical sectors are key components of the new policy.
gsSubTopics: Security challenges and their management in border areas – linkages of organized crime with terrorism.
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate.
Cyber security basics; money-laundering and its prevention.
prelimsPoints: NCIIPC released new cyber security framework for CII.
Mandates stringent security audits and threat intelligence sharing.
Aims to bolster cyber resilience against state-sponsored attacks.
Emphasis on indigenization of cyber security products.
mainsPoints: Discuss the growing threats to India's critical information infrastructure and their potential impact on national security and economy.
Analyze the key features and objectives of the new cyber security framework for CII.
Examine the challenges in implementing a robust cyber security posture across diverse critical sectors in India.
Suggest measures to enhance India's cyber security capabilities, including policy, technology, and human resource development.
GS3.imageDescription: Digital representation of interconnected networks and a lock icon, symbolizing cyber security.
GS3.trivia: The National Critical Information Infrastructure Protection Centre (NCIIPC) was established in India under Section 70A of the Information Technology Act, 2000.

GS3.headline: Government Considers New Reforms to Boost Agricultural Credit Access for Small Farmers
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers' Welfare is reportedly drafting new policy measures to enhance credit access for small and marginal farmers.
• Proposals include simplifying loan application processes, expanding the reach of institutional credit, and leveraging technology for better financial inclusion.
• Focus on reducing informal debt burdens and promoting diversified agricultural activities through targeted credit schemes.
• The reforms aim to address the persistent issue of low credit penetration for vulnerable farmer segments, especially in remote areas.
• Discussions are ongoing with public and private banks, and rural financial institutions to streamline the delivery of agricultural loans.
gsSubTopics: Major crops cropping patterns in various parts of the country, different types of irrigation and irrigation systems storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints; e-technology in the aid of farmers.

Issues related to direct and indirect farm subsidies and minimum support prices; Public Distribution System objectives, functioning, limitations, revamping; issues of buffer stocks and food security; Technology missions; economics of animal-rearing.
Food processing and related industries in India- scope’ and significance, location, upstream and downstream requirements, supply chain management.

Aims to enhance credit access for small and marginal farmers.
Proposals include simplified loan processes and tech leverage.
Focus on reducing informal debt and promoting diversification.
mainsPoints: Discuss the challenges faced by small and marginal farmers in accessing institutional credit in India.
Analyze the proposed reforms by the government to improve agricultural credit delivery and financial inclusion for farmers.
Examine the role of technology and different financial institutions in bridging the credit gap in the agricultural sector.
Evaluate the broader implications of improved credit access on farmers' income, agricultural productivity, and rural development.
GS3.imageDescription: Farmer working in a field with a tractor, symbolizing agricultural development.
GS3.trivia: The Kisan Credit Card (KCC) scheme was introduced in 1998 to provide adequate and timely credit support to farmers for their cultivation needs.

GS3.headline: India Crosses 200 GW Renewable Energy Capacity Mark, Targets Ambitious Future Growth
GS3.explainer:
• India achieves a significant milestone, surpassing 200 GW of installed renewable energy capacity (excluding large hydro).
• This achievement positions India among the top global players in renewable energy and demonstrates its commitment to climate goals.
• Solar and wind energy contribute the major share, driven by supportive government policies and declining technology costs.
• The country aims to further accelerate its renewable energy deployment to meet the ambitious target of 500 GW by 2030.
• Challenges remain in grid integration, energy storage, and ensuring a stable supply to match demand fluctuations.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
GS3.source: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE)
prelimsPoints: India's renewable energy capacity (excluding large hydro) crossed 200 GW.
Major share from solar and wind energy.
Target of 500 GW renewable energy by 2030.
Challenges in grid integration and energy storage.
mainsPoints: Discuss India's journey and achievements in scaling up its renewable energy capacity and its implications for climate change mitigation.
Analyze the key drivers and policy interventions that have facilitated the growth of solar and wind energy in the country.
Examine the challenges associated with large-scale renewable energy integration into the national grid and potential solutions.
Evaluate the economic benefits and strategic importance of India's transition to a renewable energy-dominated power sector.
GS3.imageDescription: Vast solar panel farm stretching towards the horizon, symbolizing renewable energy.
GS3.trivia: India is home to the world's largest renewable energy park, the Khavda Renewable Energy Park in Gujarat, with a planned capacity of 30 GW.

sources: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE)
